question_text: A debenture is a:
explanation: A debenture is a written acknowledgement of debt by the company. It is a DEBT instrument — the debenture holder is a CREDITOR of the company, not an owner. Debentures carry a fixed rate of interest and have a specified maturity date for repayment.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Certificate of ownership in the company
Written acknowledgement of debt by the company
Certificate of profit entitlement
Document granting voting rights

elimination_hints.hint: Ownership certificate is a share certificate. Debenture holders are creditors, not owners.
Profit entitlement belongs to shareholders. Debenture holders receive fixed interest, not profit share.
Voting rights belong to equity shareholders. Debenture holders are creditors with no voting rights.

question_text: A debenture holder is a:
explanation: A debenture holder is a CREDITOR of the company. They have lent money to the company and are entitled to receive interest at a fixed rate and repayment of principal on maturity. They are NOT owners of the company.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Owner of the company
Partner of the company
Creditor of the company
Agent of the company

elimination_hints.hint: Owners are shareholders. Debenture holders have lent money — they are creditors.
Partnership is a separate form of business. A company has shareholders and creditors, not partners.
An agent acts on behalf of the company. A debenture holder simply lends money.

question_text: Secured debentures are those which are:
explanation: Secured debentures (also called mortgage debentures) are backed by a charge on the assets of the company. If the company defaults, debenture holders can recover their money by selling the charged assets. The charge may be fixed (on specific assets) or floating (on all assets).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Backed by a charge on the assets of the company
Issued without any security or charge
Guaranteed by the government
Backed by personal guarantee of directors

elimination_hints.hint: Without security = unsecured (naked) debentures. Secured debentures have asset backing.
Government guarantee applies to government securities, not company debentures.
Personal guarantee is a separate arrangement. 'Secured' specifically means a charge on company assets.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing personal guarantee with asset charge.

question_text: Convertible debentures are those which:
explanation: Convertible debentures can be converted into equity shares or other securities after a specified period. They may be fully convertible (entire amount converts) or partly convertible (only a portion converts). This gives debenture holders the option to become shareholders.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Can be transferred from one person to another
Carry a floating rate of interest
Can be redeemed before maturity
Can be converted into equity shares after a specified period

elimination_hints.hint: Transferability relates to bearer vs registered debentures, not convertibility.
Interest rate type is unrelated to convertibility. Convertible refers to conversion into equity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing interest rate with conversion feature.

elimination_hints.hint: Early redemption is 'callable' debentures. Convertible specifically means conversion to equity shares.

question_text: Interest on debentures is:
explanation: Interest on debentures is a CHARGE against profits — it must be paid whether the company earns profit or not. It is a contractual obligation (like any loan interest). It is debited to the Statement of Profit & Loss as an expense. Dividend, in contrast, is an appropriation of profit.
subject: accountancy

options.text: A charge against profit — payable whether or not profit is earned
An appropriation of profit — payable only when profit is available
Optional payment at the discretion of the Board
Payable only on winding up of the company

elimination_hints.hint: Appropriation of profit describes dividends, not debenture interest. Interest is a mandatory expense.
Dividends are discretionary. Debenture interest is a contractual obligation — not optional.
Interest is paid periodically (annually/semi-annually), not just on winding up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking interest is only at maturity.

question_text: When debentures are issued at par for cash, the entry is:
explanation: When debentures are issued at par (face value) for cash: Bank A/c Dr. (cash received), To Debentures A/c (liability acknowledged). The Debentures Account represents the company's liability to repay the debenture holders.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debentures A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c
Bank A/c Dr. → To Debentures A/c
Bank A/c Dr. → To Share Capital A/c
Debentures A/c Dr. → To Securities Premium A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This debits Debentures (reduces liability) and credits Bank (cash goes out). But on issue, cash comes IN.
Share Capital is for shares, not debentures. Debentures create a liability, not equity.
Securities Premium relates to share issue at premium. At par debenture issue has no premium.

question_text: Registered debentures differ from bearer debentures in that:
explanation: Registered debentures are recorded in the name of the holder in the company's register. Transfer requires a formal transfer deed. Bearer debentures are transferable by mere delivery — whoever holds the debenture certificate is the owner.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Registered debentures carry higher interest rates
Bearer debentures cannot be transferred
Registered debentures require a transfer deed; bearer debentures transfer by delivery
Registered debentures are always convertible

elimination_hints.hint: Interest rate depends on terms of issue, not on whether debentures are registered or bearer.
Bearer debentures ARE transferable — in fact, they're easier to transfer (by mere delivery).
Convertibility is a separate classification. Registered debentures may or may not be convertible.

question_text: Under the Companies Act, 2013, a company can issue debentures with a maturity period not exceeding:
explanation: Under the Companies Act, 2013 (Rule 18 of Companies (Share Capital and Debentures) Rules, 2014), debentures must be redeemed within a period not exceeding 10 years from the date of issue. Infrastructure companies may issue debentures for up to 30 years.
subject: accountancy

options.text: 5 years
10 years
20 years
No maximum limit

elimination_hints.hint: 5 years is too short. The statutory limit is 10 years for regular companies.
20 years is the limit for preference share redemption, not debentures. Debentures have a 10-year limit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing debenture and preference share limits.

elimination_hints.hint: There IS a limit — 10 years for regular companies, 30 years for infrastructure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking debentures have no maturity ceiling.

question_text: Debenture holders have voting rights in the company.
explanation: FALSE. Debenture holders are creditors, not owners. They do NOT have voting rights at general meetings. Only equity shareholders (and preference shareholders in specific circumstances) have voting rights.
subtopic: No voting rights for debenture holders
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Voting is an ownership right. Debenture holders are creditors — they lend money but don't own the company.

question_text: Interest on debentures is payable even when the company incurs a loss.
explanation: TRUE. Debenture interest is a charge against profits (an expense), not an appropriation. It is a contractual obligation and must be paid regardless of whether the company makes a profit or loss. Non-payment can lead to legal action by debenture holders.
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Interest is a legal obligation — like any loan repayment. The company MUST pay regardless of profitability.

question_text: Debentures are shown under 'Shareholders' Funds' in the Balance Sheet.
explanation: FALSE. Debentures are DEBT, not equity. They are shown under 'Non-Current Liabilities' (if long-term) in the Balance Sheet. Shareholders' Funds include only Share Capital and Reserves & Surplus.
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Shareholders' Funds = equity. Debentures = debt. They appear under Non-Current Liabilities.

question_text: Debentures that are not backed by any charge on the assets of the company are called _______ debentures.
explanation: Unsecured debentures (also called naked or simple debentures) are issued without any charge on assets. The debenture holders rely solely on the company's creditworthiness. In case of default, they are treated as unsecured creditors.
subject: accountancy

question_text: Debenture interest is a _______ against profits, while dividend is an _______ of profits.
explanation: Interest on debentures is a 'charge' against profits — it's an expense that reduces profit. Dividend is an 'appropriation' of profits — it's distributed from profits after they are earned. This is a fundamental distinction between debt cost and equity return.
subtopic: Interest as charge vs dividend as appropriation
subject: accountancy

question_text:
Assertion (A): Debenture holders are paid before shareholders on winding up of a company.
Reason (R): Debenture holders are creditors and have a prior claim on the company's assets.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Creditors (including debenture holders) have priority over shareholders (owners) in asset distribution on winding up. Secured debenture holders have even higher priority as they can claim specific assets.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The creditor status is precisely why debenture holders are paid first — R directly explains A.
R is correct — debenture holders are creditors with prior claims.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the creditor status of debenture holders.

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — creditors are always paid before owners in liquidation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking shareholders have priority over creditors.

question_text:
Match the following types of debentures with their features:

| Column I (Type) | Column II (Feature) |
|---|---|
| (i) Secured debentures | (p) Transferable by mere delivery |
| (ii) Convertible debentures | (q) Backed by charge on company's assets |
| (iii) Bearer debentures | (r) No charge on assets |
| (iv) Unsecured debentures | (s) Can be converted into equity shares |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation:
(i) Secured = Backed by charge → (q)
(ii) Convertible = Can convert to equity → (s)
(iii) Bearer = Transferable by delivery → (p)
(iv) Unsecured = No charge on assets → (r)
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Secured means backed by charge (q), not no charge (r). Don't swap secured and unsecured.
Convertible converts to equity (s), not transferable by delivery (p). Bearer is the transferable one.
Secured is not 'transferable by delivery' (p). That's bearer debentures.

question_text: Which of the following is a key difference between shares and debentures?
explanation: Shares represent ownership (equity), while debentures represent borrowing (debt). Shareholders are owners who bear risk and receive dividends; debenture holders are creditors who receive fixed interest. Shareholders have voting rights; debenture holders do not. Dividends are from profits; interest is a charge.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both represent ownership of the company
Both carry fixed rates of return
Both give voting rights
Shares represent ownership; debentures represent borrowing

elimination_hints.hint: Only shares represent ownership. Debentures represent debt — the holder is a creditor, not an owner.
Debentures carry fixed interest, but equity shares have variable returns (dividends depend on profits).
Debenture holders do NOT have voting rights. Only equity shareholders do.

question_text: When debentures are issued as collateral security for a bank loan, the entry is:
explanation: When debentures are issued as collateral (secondary) security for a bank loan, only a memorandum entry is made — no journal entry in the books. The primary security is the bank loan itself. The debentures merely act as additional security. A note is given below the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank A/c Dr. → To Debentures A/c
Debentures A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c
Bank Loan A/c Dr. → To Debentures A/c
No journal entry — only a memorandum entry or note in Balance Sheet

elimination_hints.hint: This is the entry for a regular debenture issue. Collateral debentures don't create a separate liability.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating collateral as a regular issue.

elimination_hints.hint: No cash moves in a collateral arrangement. The debentures are merely pledged, not issued for cash.
No transfer between bank loan and debentures. They're independent — debentures merely back the loan.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating a link between two separate accounts.

question_text: A company issued 5,000, 10% debentures of ₹100 each at par on 1st April. Interest is payable semi-annually on 30th September and 31st March. TDS rate is 10%. The net interest paid on 30th September is:
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 is the annual interest. Semi-annual = ₹25,000. Then deduct TDS.
₹25,000 is the gross semi-annual interest. TDS of 10% (₹2,500) must be deducted.
₹45,000 = ₹50,000 − 10% TDS on annual amount. But payment is semi-annual, so use ₹25,000 as base.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying TDS to annual amount but paying semi-annual.

question_text: The entry for recording debenture interest due is:
explanation: When debenture interest becomes due: Debenture Interest A/c Dr. (expense), To Debenture Holders A/c (liability to pay). When payment is made: Debenture Holders A/c Dr., To Bank A/c (net payment), To TDS Payable A/c (tax deducted).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debenture Interest A/c Dr. → To Debenture Holders A/c
Debenture Holders A/c Dr. → To Debenture Interest A/c
Bank A/c Dr. → To Debenture Interest A/c
Debentures A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This reduces the liability (debit holders) and reduces the expense (credit interest). That's the payment entry, reversed.
No cash comes IN for interest. Interest is an expense — cash goes OUT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Showing cash receipt for an expense.

elimination_hints.hint: Debentures A/c is debited on redemption, not for interest. Interest has its own account.

question_text: Which of the following statements about debentures is INCORRECT?
explanation: The incorrect statement is that debentures can be issued at a discount without restrictions. Unlike shares (where discount is prohibited under Section 53), debentures CAN be issued at a discount. However, the discount must be written off over the life of the debenture. The statement that debentures can be issued without ANY restriction is misleading — while discount is allowed, it must be properly accounted for (written off to Statement of P&L).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debenture holders are creditors of the company
Interest on debentures is tax-deductible for the company
Debentures always carry voting rights like equity shares
Secured debentures have a charge on company assets

elimination_hints.hint: This is correct — debenture holders are indeed creditors.
This is correct — interest is an expense and is tax-deductible.
This is correct — secured debentures are backed by asset charges.
